petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physicist working in optical physics, nanomaterials, and materials science, seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under EB-1A. At the time of filing, he was a research associate in the Department of Electrical Engineering at Princeton University. He holds a Bachelor of Science degree with highest honors in applied mathematics and physics from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology (1998) and had previously won the Ukrainian National Olympiad of Young Physicists in 1993. He is self-represented on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Copy of Bachelor of Science degree in applied mathematics and physics from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, awarded 'with highest honors' in 1998; a letter from Andrey K. Sarychev (Senior Research Scientist at Purdue University's School of Electrical and Computer Engineering) affirming the petitioner was in the top two percent of his class; and a copy of his award as 'Winner of Ukrainian National Olympiad of Young Physicists' granted by the Ministry of Education in Ukraine in 1993, with the petitioner explaining less than two percent of competitors in this national high-school competition win such an award.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence documents the petitioner's academic excellence as a high school and undergraduate student, but the honors do not meet this criterion because they are scholastic awards for which only other students — not established scientists — compete. Additionally, the petitioner's undergraduate honors were internal to one academic institution and not national in scope. Finally, the honors were granted in 1993 and 1998, ten and five years before filing, and do not reflect sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Four media articles about research to which the petitioner contributed: (1) 'Nanoantennas Could Bring Sensitive Detectors, Optical Circuits' published September 3, 2002 on Photonics.com and reprinted on SpaceDaily.com, discussing left-handed materials research and quoting doctoral advisor Vladimir Shalaev (Professor at Purdue), referencing a Journal of Nonlinear Optical Physics and Materials paper co-authored by Shalaev, the petitioner, and Sarychev; (2) 'Unnatural Optics Create Precise Photonic Lens' published August 27, 2002 in EETimes, quoting Shalaev and noting he 'is assisted by Viktor Podolskiy, a postdoctoral fellow at Princeton University, and Andrey Sarychev'; (3) 'Topsy-Turvy World of Materials' published January 2003 in Materials Today, discussing collaborative work on nanowire composites and citing the petitioner's lead-authored 2002 paper; (4) 'Nanotechnology Teeny Antennas' published January 2003 in Popular Science, discussing work by 'Vladimir Shalaev and colleagues' (not naming the petitioner by name), with a letter from author Greg Mone affirming his piece was based on 'Plasmon modes in metal nanowires and left-handed materials' (Journal of Nonlinear Optical Physics and Materials, Vol. 11, No. 1, 2002, authored by V.A. Podolskiy, A.K. Sarychev, and V.M. Shalaev). On appeal, a letter from Professor Shalaev attesting the research work 'enjoyed not only professional, but also major press attention' including EE Times, Popular Science, and Materials Today.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the record contains no evidence that Photonics.com or SpaceDaily.com are professional or major trade publications. However, the record indicates EETimes is a professional or major trade publication with international circulation, and Materials Today is a scholarly publication with international circulation. The petitioner's work was discussed in four media articles, three of which appeared in professional or major trade publications with national and international circulation between 2002 and 2003, and three cited or were based on a scholarly article of which the petitioner is the lead author. The AAO concluded this evidence reflects sustained acclaim and is sufficient to meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Letter from Stanley G. Brown, Editorial Director of the American Physical Society, affirming the petitioner reviewed three manuscripts for Physical Review Letters between March 2001 and May 2003; electronic mail correspondence regarding invitation and agreement to review a manuscript for the Journal of the Optical Society of America B (but no evidence he actually reviewed it); documentation of invitation to review two grant proposals for the Petroleum Research Fund (without evidence of completion). On appeal: a second letter from Mr. Brown dated January 27, 2005, confirming the petitioner serves as referee for three APS journals (Physical Review B, Physical Review E, and Physical Review Letters) and has completed 21 reviews since March 2001, 'significantly more than the average referee' — though without indicating how many were completed before the July 5, 2003 filing date. Also 28 electronic mail messages requesting reviews for various scientific journals.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO could not consider evidence of reviews completed after the petition was filed, nor the 21 review requests made after filing, per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. The petitioner submitted evidence showing he actually completed only one of the 28 requested reviews prior to filing. Although he received numerous requests to review manuscripts and grant proposals, the record shows he had actually reviewed only three manuscripts for Physical Review Letters prior to filing. This limited service as a referee for one journal is not consistent with sustained national acclaim as a scientist.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Nine recommendation letters from scientists, five from supervisors or collaborators. Professor Shalaev's letter discusses contributions in six areas: optical response of fractal aggregates revealing giant local optical activity; percolation composites showing unique optical properties; using percolation composites as a source of attosecond energy pulses; extraordinary transmittance of light through small apertures; optical left-hand media; and deformed microcavities. Shalaev also claimed the petitioner's 'inventions in the area of nano-photonics are patented in the U.S.' (unsupported). A U.S. Patent Application 'Plasmonic Nanophotonics Methods, Materials, and Apparatuses' published March 6, 2003 (petitioner listed as co-inventor, no evidence of approval). Letter from Evgenii E. Narimanov (Assistant Professor, Princeton) describing the petitioner as 'internationally recognized' for discoveries in giant field excitations in random metal-dielectric films, ultra-fast attosecond response, and local optical activity in fractal aggregates. Letter from Gennady Shvets (Associate Professor, Illinois Institute of Technology) noting the petitioner's system is the only proposed one for left-handed media in visible frequencies. Letter from Dr. Sarychev (Purdue) calling the petitioner the 'driving force' of the left-handed media project. Letter from Francesco Stellacci (MIT) stating the petitioner's idea on optical left-handed material is 'THE ONLY ONE around.' Letter from Vladimir P. Safonov (Russian Academy of Sciences) on photomodification effect and local optical activity in fractal composites. Letter from P. Scott Carney (University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign) discussing four research areas including percolation effects, highly localized fields in fractals, anomalous transmission through periodically structured metal films, and negative index materials. Letter from Patrice Gadenne (University of Versailles) on theoretical calculations for enhanced field physics, referencing the 1999 'Langlois Prize' in France. On appeal, letter from Igor Kaganovich (PPPL Princeton) explaining the petitioner was invited to the 2003 PPPL Theoretical Physics Seminar series. Supporting articles: 'Plasmon Modes in Metal Nanowires and Left-Handed Materials' (Journal of Nonlinear Optical Physics and Materials, 2002, cited once at filing); 'Plasmon Modes and Negative Refraction in Metal Nanowire Composites' (Optics Express, 2003, no citations at filing); 'Percolation and Fractal Composites: Optical Studies' (2000, cited six times); 'Near-Field Optical Studies of Semicontinuous Metal Films' (Physical Review B, 2001, cited four times); 'Large Local Optical Activity in Fractal Aggregates of Nanoparticles' (JOSA, 2001, cited twice); 'Low-Threshold Lasing and Broad-Band Multiphoton-Excited Light Emission' (Journal of Modern Optics, 2002, zero citations); book chapter 'Fractal-Microcavity Composites: Giant Optical Responses' in Shalaev-edited Springer-Verlag volume (2002, no citations); 'Plasmon-Enhanced Absorption by Optical Phonons in Metal-Dielectric Composites' (Europhysics Letters, 2001, cited once).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted recommendation letters cannot by themselves establish eligibility because they do not demonstrate the alien's work is of major significance beyond those with whom he has worked directly, and letters solicited for immigration purposes carry less weight than preexisting independent evidence. The claim about patented inventions was unsupported — the cited document is only a patent application, not an approved patent, citing Matter of Soffici. While the record indicates the petitioner made valuable contributions, the submitted evidence shows his work has been published in scientific journals, one project was reported in four scientific periodicals, and he gave one invited talk at Princeton. However, apart from the recommendation letters' attestations, the minimal citation of the petitioner's published articles by other researchers indicates his work was not recognized as making major contributions to his field at the time of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Curriculum vitae listing 15 'refereed journal articles' and 15 'conference proceedings and other publications' co-authored by the petitioner, though the record documents only 12 of these articles and one conference manuscript. Copies of ten journal articles and one book chapter. The petitioner is lead author of three journal articles. Majority of articles published in reputable, refereed journals. Initial evidence showed nine articles and one conference paper cited a combined total of 25 times by other researchers — seven papers cited between one and two times each, most-frequently referenced article cited six times, and of three lead-authored articles only one had been cited once. On appeal, printouts from two sources showing 72 and 76 citations excluding self-citations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO stated that frequent publication is inherent to success as an established scientist and does not necessarily indicate sustained acclaim; evidence of publications must be accompanied by documentation of consistent citation by independent experts or other proof of significant impact. The appeal citation printouts could not be considered because they contain citations made after the petition was filed, per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Katigbak. Only nine of the petitioner's 14 refereed articles had been minimally cited by other researchers. At the time of filing, the petitioner was lead author of only three articles, only one of which had been cited once. This minimal citation record does not demonstrate the requisite sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Not claimed initially. On appeal, the petitioner claimed he 'has held a leading role in several [organizations]' as illustrated by his leading authorship of several scholarly publications. The petitioner also cited his current position as Assistant Professor at Oregon State University (OSU), which offered him the position on May 25, 2004.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged the petitioner played an important role on several research projects but noted that to meet this criterion, a petitioner must establish the nature of the alien's role within the entire organization and the reputation of the organization; where the role is for a section, the petitioner must establish the reputation of that section independent of the organization itself. The record did not show the petitioner held a leading or critical role for Princeton University as a research associate or for New Mexico State University as a graduate student. The record did not corroborate the alleged 'leading authorship' — at filing the petitioner had published only three articles as lead author, only one cited by one other research team. Evidence regarding the OSU position could not be considered because it arose nearly a year after the petition was filed, per Katigbak.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, holding that while the petitioner is an accomplished physicist whose work has been reported by professional scientific publications (satisfying only the published material criterion), the record does not establish that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim placing him at the very top of his field at the time of filing. Because the petitioner met only one of the required three criteria, he is ineligible for classification as an alien with extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A). The AAO emphasized that much of the evidence submitted on appeal arose after the petition was filed and could not be considered, as the petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing. The burden of proof remains with the petitioner, who has not sustained that burden. The decision was rendered without prejudice to filing a new petition with the requisite supporting documents.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: No fraud findings. Procedural issues: much of the evidence submitted on appeal arose after the petition was filed (including a Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences article, updated citation lists showing 72 and 76 citations, 21 review requests, additional completed reviews, invitations to present in 2005, and evidence of the Oregon State University Assistant Professor position offered May 25, 2004) and could not be considered under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak, which require eligibility to be established at the time of filing. Additionally, Professor Shalaev's claim that the petitioner's nano-photonics inventions were 'patented in the U.S.' was unsupported — the record only contained a patent application, not an approved patent, and the AAO cited Matter of Soffici for the principle that going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient.
